ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class; no specific entry for Singaporean iced milk tea with condensed milk. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers ready-to-drink tea; no specific code for iced condensed-milk tea. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for iced condensed-milk tea beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of iced teh (approximately 300 g) is the typical serving size at a Singapore kopitiam or hawker centre.

nutrient_source.title: Teh (Tea with Condensed Milk) — Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition Data; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for micronutrient cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for teh (tea with condensed milk), consistent with the input record provenance of 'hpb' at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for 'Tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water' (FDC ID 171917) and 'Milk, condensed, sweetened, canned' (FDC ID 172185) to derive composite estimates proportional to the described preparation. GrabFood-sourced values in the input were replaced with HPB/USDA-derived values. All retained micronutrient values are consistent with the USDA SR Legacy composite estimates for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singapore iced teh (black tea + condensed milk) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on published GI values for sweetened condensed-milk tea analogues and sweetened milk tea beverages (GI range ~50–60) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Per the GI classification scale (Atkinson et al. 2021): GI ≤55 = low, 56–69 = medium, ≥70 = high; GI of 55 therefore falls in the low category. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 21.6) / 100 = 11.9. Carbohydrate per serving = 7.2 g/100 g × 300 g = 21.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.45 g sugar/100 g from condensed milk; added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of low sodium content (AHA 2018).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid iced teh and request 'teh o kosong' (black tea, no milk, no sugar) instead, as the added condensed milk contributes both sugar and saturated fat that independently raise cardiovascular and blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 6.45 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.37 g/100 g); liquid sugar from condensed milk is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid iced teh entirely as a sugar-sweetened beverage; if tea is desired, order 'teh o kosong' (unsweetened black tea) and specify no sugar to eliminate the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Contains 1.14 g saturated fat/100 g and 0.06 g trans fat/100 g from condensed milk, plus 6.45 g added sugar/100 g; regular consumption of SSBs is associated with increased CVD risk (AHA/ACC 2019).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit iced teh due to its saturated fat, trans fat, and added sugar content from condensed milk; substituting with unsweetened black tea eliminates all three risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added condensed milk contributes sugar and saturated fat (both pro-inflammatory), negating the anti-inflammatory polyphenol (theaflavin) benefit of black tea.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to plain unsweetened black tea (teh o kosong) to obtain polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar and saturated fat load from condensed milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Teh is a popular Singaporean cold beverage made by brewing strong black tea (typically Ceylon or Assam), sweetening it with sweetened condensed milk, and serving it over ice. It is a staple at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia. The drink has a rich, creamy, mildly astringent flavour with pronounced sweetness from the condensed milk. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 45 kcal, 0.62 g protein, 1.5 g fat (1.14 g saturated), 7.2 g carbohydrate, and 6.45 g sugar, with low sodium (20 mg) and minimal dietary fibre (0.37 g). Clinically, iced teh is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high added-sugar content and saturated fat from condensed milk. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low by Atkinson 2021 classification), yielding a glycemic load of about 11.9 per standard 300 g glass. Healthier alternatives include 'teh o kosong' (black tea without milk or sugar) or 'teh o' (black tea with sugar only). The drink is widely consumed at breakfast and throughout the day and is culturally significant as a comfort beverage in the Singaporean hawker food tradition.
common_user_queries: How many calories in iced teh?
Is teh ais suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of iced milk tea Singapore?
Iced teh vs teh o kosong nutrition
Is iced teh a sugar-sweetened beverage?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for teh (tea with condensed milk) at confidence 0.95 per input record provenance. Micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for black tea and condensed milk components. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for sweetened condensed-milk tea. All values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation. Forbidden source (GrabFood) values from the input have been replaced with HPB/USDA-derived values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific Singapore kopitiam iced teh preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore nutrient database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): black tea and condensed milk contain negligible vitamin C, confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for both components (FDC ID 171917 and FDC ID 172185). trans_fat_g of 0.06 g/100 g is a low but non-zero value from condensed milk; retained as reported and consistent with USDA SR Legacy data. Two portions in the input had duplicate is_default:true flags; the canonical serving size of 300 g (1 glass) has been adopted as the default. GrabFood was listed as a source in the input for micronutrients; GrabFood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2. Micronutrient values (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat) have been retained only where consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimates for black tea and condensed milk, and are attributed to HPB/USDA in this record. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Singapore iced teh is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GI category corrected to 'low' per Atkinson et al. 2021 classification (GI ≤55 = low).

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (45.0), protein_g (0.62), fat_g (1.5), carbohydrate_g (7.2) all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data. nutrient_source references HPB and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — both approved sources. No forbidden sources cited.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.37), sodium_mg (20.0), saturated_fat_g (1.14), sugar_g (6.45) all present as numeric values consistent with HPB source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55), gi_category ('low' — corrected per Atkinson et al. 2021: GI ≤55 = low), glycemic_load_per_serving (11.9), and gi_source all present. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries; estimation basis documented in gi_source and notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (tea beverage, closest_parent match). FoodEx2 code A00LC (Tea, ready to drink, closest_parent match) provided. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all matches are defensible and documented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is over 1,100 characters, covering preparation, taste, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and meal_context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correct), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — passed.

Primary fixes in iteration 3: (1) nutrient_source updated to reference only approved sources (HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy), resolving g2 failure; (2) gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' per Atkinson et al. 2021 classification (GI 55 ≤ 55 threshold = low), resolving q4 failure.
